ACME CO BOARD MEETING TRANSCRIPT — Q1 2026 REVIEW

Date: 14 April 2026
Location: Acme Co HQ, Boardroom 2 (with three directors dialled in)
Attendees: Customer Q (CEO), Customer R (CFO), Customer S (Chair), Customer T (NED), Customer U (NED, dialled in), Customer V (NED, dialled in), Customer W (Investor Director, dialled in), with Customer X (Company Secretary) taking minutes.

Customer S opened the meeting at 09:32 and confirmed quoracy. The minutes of the previous meeting on 22 January 2026 were approved without amendment and signed by the Chair.

Q1 FINANCIAL REVIEW

Customer R walked the board through the Q1 numbers. Group revenue for the three months to 31 March 2026 came in at 8.4 million pounds, against a forecast of 8.0 million pounds and prior-year comparable of 6.9 million pounds. The 5 percent overshoot against forecast was driven primarily by stronger-than-expected renewals in the mid-market segment, with 94 percent gross retention against a planned 91 percent. New business closed at 1.2 million pounds of new annual recurring revenue, very slightly behind the 1.3 million pound plan, with the shortfall concentrated in February.

Gross margin held at 73 percent, in line with plan. Operating costs were 5.6 million pounds against a 5.7 million pound budget, with the underspend coming almost entirely from delayed hiring in the engineering organisation. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was 0.5 million pounds, ahead of the 0.1 million pound plan, though Customer R cautioned the board that the EBITDA beat was largely a timing effect and would unwind across Q2 and Q3 as the delayed hires came on board.

Cash at the end of the quarter stood at 11.2 million pounds, with no draw on the 5 million pound revolving credit facility. Customer R reminded the board that the facility comes up for renewal in October 2026 and that initial conversations with the lender had been constructive.

Customer T asked whether the renewal strength was sustainable or whether it reflected a pull-forward of customers who would otherwise have churned later in the year. Customer R said the analysis suggested it was largely sustainable but that he would bring a fuller cohort view to the May meeting.

HIRING PLAN

Customer Q presented the updated 2026 hiring plan. The original full-year plan called for 42 net new hires across the business; the revised plan proposes 36, with the reduction concentrated in marketing and sales operations. Engineering hiring remains at 18 net new hires for the year, of which 4 had joined in Q1. The board noted that the engineering team had carried roughly 6 unfilled roles for most of Q1, which Customer Q attributed primarily to a tighter London market for senior backend engineers and secondarily to a deliberate decision to raise the seniority bar on two of the open roles.

Customer V asked whether the reduced marketing hiring would put the new business plan at risk. Customer Q replied that the marketing reduction was made possible by a shift in channel mix toward partner-sourced pipeline, which now accounts for roughly 38 percent of new business pipeline against 22 percent a year ago. Customer V accepted this but asked for the partner pipeline numbers to be tracked as a standing board KPI from Q2 onward. The board agreed.

Customer W asked about attrition. Customer Q reported that voluntary attrition for the trailing twelve months was 11 percent, down from 14 percent a year ago, with engineering attrition at 8 percent. Customer W said this was a healthy number and complimented the people team.

STRATEGIC DECISION: GERMAN MARKET ENTRY

The principal item of business was the proposed entry into the German market in H2 2026. Customer Q presented the case, supported by a 28-page board paper that had been circulated the previous week. The proposal was to open a small Berlin office in October 2026, with an initial team of one country manager, two account executives, one solutions engineer, and one customer success manager, at a fully loaded annual cost of approximately 1.1 million pounds.

The rationale rested on three points. First, inbound demand from German prospects had grown roughly threefold over the previous twelve months, with 41 inbound qualified leads in Q1 2026 against 13 in Q1 2025. Second, the company's largest existing customer in the DACH region, Customer A, had indicated it would expand its contract significantly if local-language support and a German billing entity were available. Third, two of the company's three main competitors had opened German operations in the previous eighteen months, and Customer Q argued that further delay would cede the market.

Customer T raised concerns about the cost. With the revised hiring plan already tightening the operating model, an additional 1.1 million pounds of annualised cost in H2 represented roughly a 5 percent uplift on the full-year cost base. Customer R confirmed that the German investment had not been included in the budget approved in January and would need to be funded from the EBITDA upside or accepted as a planned reduction in full-year EBITDA.

Customer U, dialling in, asked whether a partner-led entry — using a local reseller rather than direct hires — had been considered. Customer Q said it had, and the board paper covered the analysis on pages 14 to 19, but the conclusion was that a reseller model would not unlock the Customer A expansion, which required a direct contractual relationship with a German entity. Customer U accepted the answer but asked that the partner-led option be kept under review for the Austrian and Swiss markets.

Customer S then put the proposal to a vote. The board approved the German market entry unanimously, with the following conditions: (1) the country manager hire must be approved by the Chair and CEO jointly before the offer is extended; (2) Customer R will bring a revised full-year forecast incorporating the German investment to the May board meeting; (3) a six-month review of the German operation will be tabled at the April 2027 board meeting.

OTHER BUSINESS

Customer R briefly updated the board on the audit, which is on track for sign-off in late June. There were no significant findings from the interim audit work.

Customer Q noted that the senior leadership offsite would take place in Lisbon in the first week of June, and invited any director who wished to attend the Friday strategy session to confirm with the Company Secretary by mid-May.

The meeting closed at 12:48. The next scheduled meeting is 19 May 2026.

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) the Q1 financial result, including revenue of 8.4 million pounds against an 8.0 million pound forecast and the EBITDA beat; (2) the revised hiring plan reducing full-year hires from 42 to 36 with engineering hiring held at 18; (3) the unanimous board decision to approve entry into the German market in H2 2026 with a Berlin office. Must accurately state: revenue figure, EBITDA position, that engineering hiring was held flat, and that the German entry was approved unanimously rather than merely discussed. Must NOT: claim the company entered Germany via a reseller (the reseller option was rejected for Germany); state that attrition rose (it fell from 14 to 11 percent); or attribute the German decision to any single director rather than a unanimous board vote.

UK SaaS Pricing Trends in 2026: The Quiet Repricing of the Mid-Market

Published in SaaS Quarterly UK, April 2026 edition

For most of the past decade, the conventional wisdom in UK software-as-a-service pricing was simple: per-seat pricing was the default, annual contracts were the goal, and discounting was the lever pulled when growth slowed. That picture has shifted noticeably over the last twelve months, and the shift is most visible in the mid-market segment serving UK companies between 50 and 500 employees.

A survey of 184 UK-headquartered SaaS vendors conducted by SaaS Quarterly UK between January and March 2026 found that 61 percent had adjusted their published pricing in the previous twelve months, up from 38 percent in the equivalent survey a year earlier. Of those that had repriced, just over half — 53 percent — had introduced some form of usage-linked component to a list price that had previously been purely per-seat. A further 22 percent had moved to a hybrid model combining a platform fee with seat-based access, and 18 percent had adjusted seat prices upward without changing the underlying model. Only 7 percent had reduced headline prices.

The drivers cited by respondents were mixed. The most common, named by 71 percent of vendors who had repriced, was the rising cost of serving customers — a category that in 2026 is dominated by the cost of model inference for AI-enabled features. Vendors reported that AI features which had been introduced in 2024 and 2025 as bundled enhancements were proving materially more expensive to operate than originally modelled, and that the gap between contracted seat revenue and per-customer serving cost had narrowed uncomfortably. The second most common driver, cited by 49 percent, was a desire to better align price with the value customers were realising, particularly where AI features had measurably reduced the customer's need for additional seats.

A third driver, less often named but more strategically significant, was the difficulty of growing accounts under pure per-seat pricing as customer headcount itself stagnated or declined. Several vendors interviewed for this article — speaking on background — reported that their largest customers were holding seat counts flat or reducing them year-on-year, even where product engagement was rising. Under a per-seat model, this dynamic translates directly into flat or declining account revenue, regardless of how much value the product is delivering. A move to usage-based or hybrid pricing breaks that link.

The response from buyers has been less uniform than vendors might have hoped. Procurement teams at mid-market UK companies have grown noticeably more sophisticated over the past two years, and several procurement leaders interviewed for this piece reported that they now routinely model three- and five-year cost trajectories under proposed pricing structures before signing. Where a vendor proposes a usage-linked component, buyers are increasingly insisting on contractual caps, predictability mechanisms, or the right to revert to a fixed-fee structure if usage exceeds defined thresholds. One procurement director at a mid-sized UK financial services firm described the dynamic as "a slow renegotiation of who carries the AI cost risk."

Vendors that have moved fastest on repricing are not, in the main, the largest. The survey data suggests that vendors with annual recurring revenue between 5 million and 25 million pounds were the most likely to have repriced in the past year, with 74 percent reporting a change. Vendors above 100 million pounds in ARR were the least likely, at 41 percent. The pattern is consistent with the view, expressed by several investors interviewed, that smaller vendors face less customer-base inertia and can move pricing more quickly without triggering churn at scale.

The sectoral picture is also uneven. Vendors selling into the legal, financial services, and professional services sectors were considerably more likely to have repriced than vendors selling into education, healthcare, or the public sector. The survey attributes this gap primarily to procurement cycle length and the prevalence of multi-year contracts in the slower-moving sectors, which delay the point at which a vendor can practically introduce a new pricing structure to existing customers.

Discounting practices have also shifted. The proportion of new business closed at a discount of 20 percent or more from list price fell from 44 percent in Q1 2025 to 31 percent in Q1 2026, according to data shared with SaaS Quarterly UK by three benchmarking providers. The shift is partly attributed to tighter sales discipline as vendors protect repriced list prices, and partly to the introduction of usage-based components which themselves are rarely discounted in the same way as seat fees. Annual contract values, meanwhile, have risen modestly — the median new mid-market deal size grew from approximately 28,000 pounds in Q1 2025 to approximately 33,000 pounds in Q1 2026 — though this growth is concentrated in vendors that have introduced usage-based or hybrid pricing.

Not all vendors are convinced that the shift toward usage-based pricing is durable. A minority view, expressed most clearly by the founder of one bootstrapped UK vendor with around 12 million pounds of ARR, is that usage-based pricing simply transfers revenue volatility from the vendor to the customer's procurement department, and that the resulting friction will eventually push the market back toward predictable, capacity-based pricing. This view remains a minority one, but it is held with some conviction by a subset of founders who built their businesses on the predictability of annual per-seat contracts.

The regulatory environment has played a smaller role than might be expected. Although the UK Competition and Markets Authority has spent considerable energy on cloud infrastructure pricing during 2025 and 2026, its work has not directly touched mid-market SaaS pricing structures. Several vendors interviewed expressed mild concern that the CMA's posture could broaden, particularly in the area of AI-feature bundling and the transparency of usage-linked charges, but no respondent reported having altered pricing in direct response to regulatory pressure.

Looking ahead, the survey asked vendors whether they expected to make further pricing changes in the next twelve months. 47 percent said they did, and of those, the largest single group — 38 percent — said they expected to introduce or expand usage-based components. A further 26 percent said they expected to raise headline seat prices. Only 9 percent said they expected to reduce prices in any form, and the comments accompanying these responses suggested that planned reductions were targeted at specific customer segments — typically the smallest tier — rather than broad-based.

The overall picture is of a UK SaaS market that is repricing itself with more deliberation than at any point in the past five years. The shift is not yet a wholesale move away from per-seat pricing, but it is a clear and broad-based move toward pricing models that decouple revenue from headcount and that allow vendors to recover the rising costs of serving AI-enabled features. The vendors that navigate this transition most successfully are likely to be those that combine clear value narratives with pricing structures that buyers find genuinely predictable — a balance that, on the evidence of the past twelve months, remains harder to strike than many vendors had assumed.

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) the broad shift among UK mid-market SaaS vendors toward usage-based or hybrid pricing, with 61 percent having repriced in the previous twelve months; (2) the principal drivers, especially rising AI inference costs and the desire to break the per-seat-to-revenue link; (3) the buyer-side response, particularly increased procurement sophistication and demands for caps or predictability mechanisms. Must accurately state: that the survey covered 184 vendors, that 61 percent had repriced (not all of them), and that smaller vendors (5–25 million pound ARR) were faster to reprice than larger ones. Must NOT: claim a wholesale abandonment of per-seat pricing (it is described as a partial shift); claim that the CMA drove the changes (regulatory pressure was explicitly described as not a driver); or invent specific named companies.

metadata.notes: Tests summarisation of an industry article with multiple statistics and a nuanced thesis the model must capture without overstating the trend.

BRIGHTLINE LOGISTICS — Q4 2025 OPERATIONS REVIEW

Prepared for the executive team
Reporting period: 1 October to 31 December 2025
Issued: 22 January 2026
Author: Customer F, VP Operations

1. EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW

Q4 2025 was the busiest quarter in Brightline Logistics' history, driven by the usual seasonal peak in November and December and amplified by the onboarding of three significant new accounts in October. Total parcel volume processed across the network was 14.7 million units, up 23 percent on Q4 2024 and 9 percent ahead of the internal forecast set in August. On-time delivery performance held at 94.1 percent against a target of 95.0 percent, a small miss that nonetheless represents a significant improvement on the 91.3 percent recorded in Q4 2024. Fleet utilisation averaged 87 percent across the quarter, in line with plan. The new East Midlands cross-dock facility went live on 6 November as scheduled.

2. VOLUME

Volume by month: October 4.1 million parcels, November 5.0 million parcels, December 5.6 million parcels. The month-on-month growth pattern was steeper than usual, reflecting both the new account onboardings and a longer pre-Christmas peak driven by retail customers extending promotional periods further into early November. Black Friday week alone accounted for 1.4 million parcels, which is the highest single-week volume the network has ever processed.

By customer segment: e-commerce retail accounted for 71 percent of total volume, B2B distribution 21 percent, and returns processing 8 percent. The returns segment grew 34 percent year-on-year, the fastest of the three, driven by the broader market shift toward extended returns windows over the Christmas trading period.

By region, the South East and London corridor remained the largest contributor at 38 percent of volume, followed by the Midlands at 24 percent, the North West at 17 percent, the South West at 11 percent, and the rest of the network at 10 percent. The East Midlands cross-dock contributed approximately 0.8 million parcels in its partial first quarter of operation.

Three new accounts onboarded in October collectively contributed approximately 1.6 million parcels of Q4 volume. The largest of these, Customer A, onboarded on 7 October and ramped to full forecast volume by the end of the month, slightly ahead of plan. Customer B onboarded on 14 October and reached full volume in the second week of November, three weeks behind plan; the delay was attributable to integration issues with Customer B's order management system that took longer than expected to resolve. Customer C onboarded on 28 October and ramped on schedule.

3. ON-TIME DELIVERY PERFORMANCE

On-time delivery for the quarter was 94.1 percent, calculated as the proportion of parcels delivered by the customer-facing promised date. Performance was strongest in October at 95.4 percent, dipped in November to 93.6 percent during the peak ramp, and recovered partially in December to 93.7 percent.

The November dip was concentrated in the South East corridor, where on-time performance fell to 91.2 percent for the month. Root-cause analysis identified three contributing factors: (i) an unplanned absence rate among drivers in the Dartford depot that ran at 14 percent for the first three weeks of November, against a normal 8 percent; (ii) the integration issues with Customer B's order management system, which produced a backlog of mis-routed parcels in the second week of November; and (iii) two days of severe weather in the week of 17 November that disrupted last-mile delivery across the South East.

December on-time performance was held back primarily by the volume of returns flowing back through the network in the week of 22 December, which placed unexpected pressure on the East Midlands cross-dock during its first peak period. The operations team has identified specific process changes for Q1 2026 to better handle returns peaks.

Claims and complaints volume rose roughly in line with parcel volume, at 21 percent year-on-year against the 23 percent volume increase, suggesting that quality of service held broadly stable on a per-parcel basis. The most common complaint category remained delivery time, accounting for 47 percent of complaints, followed by parcel condition at 23 percent and missed delivery slots at 18 percent.

4. FLEET

The quarter saw the planned addition of 42 new vehicles to the fleet, bringing the total active fleet to 587 vehicles at quarter-end. The new vehicles were a mix of 28 small delivery vans and 14 medium rigid trucks, all electric or plug-in hybrid. The transition continues to track toward the published 2027 target of a 70 percent zero-emission fleet, with the proportion now at 51 percent at the end of Q4 2025, up from 44 percent at the end of Q3.

Fleet utilisation averaged 87 percent across the quarter, against a plan of 86 percent. Utilisation was highest in November at 91 percent and dipped in late December as volumes tapered.

The new East Midlands cross-dock facility, which went live on 6 November, is now operating at approximately 65 percent of its design throughput. The ramp to full design throughput is planned over the first half of 2026. The facility cost approximately 18 million pounds to build and equip, marginally below the 19 million pound budget approved by the board in early 2024. Customer F notes that the facility's primary near-term contribution is to reduce trunking distance for parcels originating in the Midlands and destined for the South East, with a measured fuel saving in November and December of approximately 4 percent on the affected lanes.

The Q4 expansion of the fleet was accompanied by the recruitment of 67 new drivers, partially offsetting attrition of 39 drivers across the quarter. Driver retention remains a significant operational focus, and the people team is leading a review of driver compensation and shift patterns that will be presented to the executive team in February.

5. COSTS AND MARGIN

Operating costs for the quarter were 41.2 million pounds, against a budget of 40.5 million pounds. The 0.7 million pound overrun was driven primarily by higher-than-budgeted agency driver costs in November (0.5 million pounds) and the costs of resolving the Customer B integration issue (0.2 million pounds). Fuel costs were broadly in line with plan, helped by the fleet electrification transition.

6. OUTLOOK FOR Q1 2026

Q1 is traditionally the quietest quarter, but Brightline expects volume of approximately 11.0 million parcels, up 18 percent on Q1 2025, reflecting the full-quarter contribution of the three new accounts onboarded in Q4. The East Midlands cross-dock will continue ramping toward design throughput, and the operations team will focus on returning South East on-time performance to above 95 percent.

7. CONCLUSION

Q4 2025 was a record quarter by volume, delivered with on-time performance materially better than the prior year despite a small miss against the internal target. The new East Midlands facility came online to plan and is already contributing to network efficiency. The principal operational risk identified by the review is driver availability in the South East, which the executive team will discuss further in the February operations review.

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) record Q4 volume of 14.7 million parcels (up 23 percent year-on-year) including the onboarding of three new accounts; (2) on-time delivery performance of 94.1 percent — a small miss against the 95 percent target but a meaningful improvement on Q4 2024's 91.3 percent; (3) the fleet expansion of 42 new vehicles and the on-schedule launch of the East Midlands cross-dock facility on 6 November. Must accurately state: the volume figure, the on-time percentages, and that the cross-dock launched on schedule rather than late. Must NOT: claim Brightline hit its on-time target (it missed by 0.9 points); claim the cross-dock was delayed or over budget (it launched on plan and slightly under budget); or attribute the November on-time dip to a single cause when three were named.

ACME CO BILLING WEBHOOK SYSTEM — HANDOVER DOCUMENT

From: Customer M, Senior Engineer (departing 30 April 2026)
To: Customer N, incoming owner; Customer O, secondary on-call
Last updated: 24 April 2026

This document is the handover for the billing webhook system that I have owned since mid-2024. It is intended to be read end to end before you take pager duty for the service. If anything below is unclear, please ask before 30 April; after that I will not be reachable.

1. WHAT THE SYSTEM DOES

The billing webhook system is the bridge between our payment processor (referred to throughout as the PSP) and our internal billing database. The PSP emits webhook events when payments succeed, fail, are refunded, or when subscription state changes. Our system receives these events, validates them, and applies the corresponding state changes to the billing database. Roughly 95 percent of events are payment-success events; the remainder are split across failures, refunds, disputes, and subscription lifecycle events.

At steady state the system processes between 30,000 and 80,000 events per day, with peaks of around 200,000 on the first business day of each month when most subscription renewals fire.

2. ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW

The system is a small Python service running on three pods in the production Kubernetes cluster, behind an internal load balancer. The PSP posts events to a single HTTPS endpoint (`/webhooks/psp`), which is exposed via the public ingress. The service performs the following steps for each event:

  (a) Verifies the PSP's HMAC signature on the request body, using the shared secret stored in the `psp-webhook-secret` Kubernetes secret in the `billing` namespace.
  (b) Writes the raw event to the `webhook_events_raw` table in the billing Postgres database, with a unique constraint on the PSP's event ID. Duplicate event IDs are silently dropped at this stage; the PSP retries aggressively and duplicates are common.
  (c) Enqueues a job onto the `webhook-processing` queue for asynchronous processing.
  (d) Returns a 200 response to the PSP within the PSP's required 10-second window.

A worker pool consumes the `webhook-processing` queue and applies the event to the billing database. Worker code lives in the same repository as the webhook receiver. Failed jobs are retried with exponential backoff up to six times, after which they are moved to a dead-letter queue and an alert fires.

3. CODE LOCATION

The service lives in the monorepo at `services/billing-webhooks/`. The receiver is in `receiver.py`, the worker is in `worker.py`, the database models are in `models.py`, and the PSP signature verification is in `signing.py`. Tests are in `tests/`. The CI pipeline runs the test suite on every pull request and blocks merges on failures.

4. KNOWN QUIRKS AND GOTCHAS

There are several things about this system that have caused incidents in the past. Please read this section carefully.

4.1 The PSP's event ordering is not guaranteed. In particular, a `subscription.updated` event can arrive before the `subscription.created` event for the same subscription. The worker handles this by buffering update events for up to five minutes if the corresponding create event has not yet been applied. This logic is in `worker.py` around line 240. If you see a spike in `webhook_buffered_events` on the dashboard, this is the mechanism activating.

4.2 The PSP occasionally re-sends events from up to 30 days in the past, typically after their own internal incidents. The unique constraint in `webhook_events_raw` handles deduplication for events we have already seen. However, if an event from 30 days ago arrives that we have never seen — which has happened twice in the past year — the worker will attempt to apply it as if it were current. This has caused at least one billing anomaly. There is a TODO in `worker.py` to add a configurable maximum-age check; this is not yet implemented and would be a reasonable first improvement for the new owner.

4.3 The HMAC signature verification is sensitive to whitespace in the request body. We had an outage in October 2025 when a routine ingress upgrade introduced trailing-newline normalisation that broke signature verification. The fix was to verify the signature against the raw body bytes before any decoding. Do not refactor `signing.py` without rerunning the integration tests against a real PSP test event.

4.4 The billing database has a row-level lock on the `subscriptions` table that the worker takes when applying subscription state changes. Under very high concurrency (which we have seen only once, during the November 2025 peak) this can cause worker timeouts. The mitigation in place is to limit the worker pool concurrency to 8 per pod, configured in `values.yaml`. Do not increase this without load testing.

4.5 The dead-letter queue alert fires on any message in the queue. In practice it fires roughly once a fortnight, almost always for genuine reasons that need a human to look at. Please do not silence this alert. If a message in the dead-letter queue requires reprocessing, the runbook is at `docs/runbooks/billing-webhook-dlq.md`.

5. DEPENDENCIES AND OWNERSHIP BOUNDARIES

The service depends on the billing Postgres database (owned by the platform data team), the internal queue infrastructure (owned by the platform infrastructure team), and the PSP itself (owned externally). The PSP account credentials and webhook configuration are managed by the finance operations team; if the webhook URL or signing secret needs to change, you will need to coordinate with Customer P in finance ops.

The service is consumed by the billing service proper, which reads from the `subscriptions` and `invoices` tables that this service writes to. Schema changes to those tables must be coordinated with the billing service owner, currently Customer R.

6. ON-CALL AND ALERTS

The service has four production alerts:

  (i) Webhook receiver error rate above 1 percent for five minutes. Usually indicates a PSP outage or a deployment issue.
  (ii) Worker queue depth above 5,000. Usually indicates a worker problem; check pod status first.
  (iii) Dead-letter queue message count above zero. As above, do not silence.
  (iv) End-to-end webhook latency (receipt to database write) above 60 seconds at the 95th percentile.

The primary on-call engineer should be the new owner; the secondary should be a member of the billing team rotation. I have already updated the on-call schedule for May.

7. RECOMMENDED FIRST IMPROVEMENTS

Three things I would do first if I were staying:

  (a) Implement the maximum-age check on inbound events (see 4.2).
  (b) Add structured logging on the buffering logic so that 4.1 is easier to debug live.
  (c) Improve the test coverage on `worker.py`, which sits at around 64 percent line coverage. The branches that handle the unusual event types are particularly thin.

8. CONTACT

I will be reachable on the team Slack channel until 30 April. After that, please escalate to Customer R for billing-service questions, Customer P for PSP-account questions, and the platform infrastructure team for queue or cluster questions.

Good luck.

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) what the system does — receives PSP webhook events and applies them to the billing database, with steady-state volume of 30,000–80,000 events per day and monthly peaks; (2) the architectural shape — receiver verifies HMAC signature, persists raw event, enqueues for asynchronous worker processing with retries and a dead-letter queue; (3) at least one of the major quirks/gotchas (event ordering, late-arriving old events, HMAC whitespace sensitivity, or worker concurrency limits) that the new owner needs to be aware of. Must accurately state: that the service is asynchronous (receiver returns quickly, worker processes later) and that the dead-letter queue alert should not be silenced. Must NOT: claim the system is synchronous; invent technologies not mentioned (e.g. claim it uses Kafka or AWS SQS); or attribute ownership of the PSP credentials to anyone other than the finance operations team.

metadata.notes: Tests summarisation of a technical handover where the model must extract the architectural shape and at least one operational gotcha rather than getting lost in the line-by-line detail.

Subject: April monthly update — wins, hires, and where we're focused
From: Customer Q, CEO, Acme Co
To: All hands
Sent: 28 April 2026

Hi everyone,

It is the end of April, which means it is time for the monthly update. As ever, the goal of these notes is to give you a single, honest read on where the company is, what is going well, what is not, and what we are focused on for the month ahead. If anything in here is unclear or you want to dig in further, please bring it to the all-hands on Thursday or grab me in Slack.

A QUICK NUMBERS RECAP

April was a strong month commercially. We closed 1.3 million pounds of new annual recurring revenue, which is the best single month we have ever had, narrowly beating the previous record set in March 2025. New logos accounted for roughly 60 percent of that figure and expansion of existing accounts the remaining 40 percent. Year to date we are now at 4.2 million pounds of new ARR, comfortably ahead of the 3.7 million pound year-to-date plan.

Gross retention for the trailing twelve months sits at 93 percent and net retention at 112 percent. Both are healthy, both are slightly ahead of plan, and both reflect the very deliberate work the customer success team has been doing on early-warning signals over the past two quarters. Thank you to that team in particular.

Cash at the end of April was 11.6 million pounds. We have not drawn on the credit facility and have no plans to do so this year.

WINS WORTH CALLING OUT

Three wins from the month deserve a specific mention.

First, we closed Customer A as a new logo on 17 April. This is the largest single new-logo contract in the company's history at 480,000 pounds of ARR. The deal was led by Customer B in sales with strong support from Customer C in solutions engineering and Customer D in legal. Customer A is a marquee name in their sector and we expect their reference value to be material as we go after similar accounts in H2.

Second, the renewal of Customer E completed on 22 April with a 35 percent uplift, taking that account from 240,000 pounds to 324,000 pounds of ARR. This was a particularly hard renewal — Customer E has been with us for four years and went into the renewal cycle with two competitive evaluations actively running. Customer F led the renewal and the customer success team's work on demonstrating realised value over the past twelve months was decisive.

Third, the partnerships team signed a co-selling agreement with Customer G on 9 April. We do not yet know what this will be worth, but the early signals from the joint pipeline workshop two weeks ago are encouraging — roughly 1.4 million pounds of qualified pipeline identified in a single afternoon. Customer H deserves a lot of credit for getting this over the line; it has been in negotiation since November.

HIRES

We welcomed seven new joiners this month, which is the largest single-month intake of the year so far. Specifically: two senior backend engineers (Customer I and Customer J, joining the platform team), one solutions engineer (Customer K, joining the pre-sales team), one customer success manager (Customer L, joining the mid-market team), one product manager (Customer M, joining the analytics product team), and two account executives (Customer N and Customer O, joining the new-business team).

All seven joiners are based in the UK and all seven came through our standard hiring process. I want to call out specifically the work the people team has done on reducing time-to-offer; we are now at a median of 21 days from first conversation to offer accepted, down from 34 days at the start of the year. That improvement matters: it is making us competitive for candidates we would have lost six months ago.

We still have eleven open roles, the most pressing of which are a senior product manager for the integrations area, a head of demand generation, and a German-speaking country manager (more on that below).

WHERE WE ARE FOCUSED IN MAY

Three areas of focus for the month ahead.

The first is the German market entry, which the board approved at the April meeting. We are targeting an October opening of the Berlin office. The single most important decision in the next six weeks is the country manager hire; Customer P is leading the search and we already have three strong candidates in the process. Everything else flows from getting that hire right.

The second is the platform reliability initiative. Some of you will have seen the post-mortem from the 11 April incident, when our European customers experienced roughly 90 minutes of degraded performance during the morning peak. The root cause was a combination of a poorly-tuned database query and an autoscaler configuration that did not respond quickly enough to the load. The platform team has a clear remediation plan and the headline commitment is that we will return our 99.9 percent monthly availability target by the end of May. Customer R is leading this and will be sharing weekly updates in the engineering channel.

The third is the H2 planning cycle, which kicks off in earnest in mid-May. The senior leadership offsite in Lisbon (first week of June) will produce the H2 plan. Before that, every team should be doing the work to bring its own H2 priorities and resourcing requests to the table. Your manager will have more detail on what is expected from your team in the next few days.

A NOTE ON THE PRODUCT ROADMAP

We have not historically been good at communicating roadmap clearly across the whole company, and that is something I want to fix this quarter. The product team is working on a new internal roadmap view that will be shared at the May all-hands. The headline is that the analytics product line is the area we are investing most heavily in for H2, with the integrations product line a close second. The core platform will continue to receive the engineering investment it needs but will not see major new feature work in H2.

A THANK YOU

This is the strongest month we have had in some time. The numbers are good, the wins are good, and the team is in good shape. None of that is accidental and none of it is one person's doing. Thank you for the work you put in to make April what it was.

If you want to talk about anything in this update — or anything else — my calendar is open and the all-hands on Thursday is the right place to raise the bigger questions.

More in May.

Customer Q

Must accurately state: that 1.3 million pounds was the new ARR figure and that the German market entry was already approved by the board (not still under discussion). Must NOT: claim the company has drawn on the credit facility (it has not); attribute the Customer A win to a single named individual when several were credited; or invent product roadmap details beyond the analytics-and-integrations focus stated.

Subject: Small reorg in the operations group — effective 12 May
From: Customer Q, CEO
To: All hands
Sent: 28 April 2026

Hi everyone,

This is a short note to flag a small but useful change to how the operations group is structured. It takes effect on 12 May. Nothing about this change affects the commercial or product organisations.

The headline is straightforward. We are bringing the workplace, IT, and business-systems teams together into a single Operations group, reporting to Customer X, our new VP of Operations. Customer X joined us at the start of April and most of you will have met them at the new-joiner welcome two weeks ago; they spent the first month doing listening sessions across the affected teams before we settled on the new shape.

The rationale is that these three teams have been working closely together for the last year on a common set of internal-tooling and workplace projects, and the existing reporting lines — workplace into the COO's office, IT into the CTO's office, business systems into finance — have been a source of friction in getting decisions made quickly. Bringing them together under one leader removes that friction and also gives us a single point of accountability for the internal employee experience, which is something several of you have asked for in the engagement surveys.

WHAT IS CHANGING

The people moves are small. The workplace team (currently four people, led by Customer Y) keeps the same scope and team but moves reporting line from the COO to Customer X. The IT team (currently six people, led by Customer Z) keeps the same scope and team but moves reporting line from the CTO to Customer X. The business systems team (currently three people, led by Customer AA) keeps the same scope and team but moves reporting line from the CFO to Customer X.

No roles are being eliminated and no one's job is changing in substance. The leaders of the three sub-teams continue in their current roles and report to Customer X. There are no changes to compensation, location, or working patterns associated with this reorg.

WHAT IS NOT CHANGING

For the avoidance of doubt: the operations function as defined here is purely internal — workplace, IT, business systems. Customer X's remit does not extend to commercial operations, product operations, or any customer-facing function. Those continue to report as they do today. If you previously raised an internal-tooling request through the IT team or a workplace request through the workplace team, you continue to do so through the same channels; only the reporting line above the team lead is changing.

The COO, CTO, and CFO have all been involved in shaping this change and are supportive of it. They are also the right people to talk to if you have questions about why a particular team has moved out of their reporting line.

WHY NOW

We have known for several months that this consolidation would be useful, but we wanted to wait until we had the right person in place to lead it. With Customer X now four weeks into the role and having completed their initial listening, this is the right moment.

A few of you have asked whether this signals a broader reorganisation. It does not. There are no other reporting-line changes planned in the next quarter. If that changes I will tell you directly; you will not learn it from a rumour.

WHAT TO EXPECT

In the first 90 days after 12 May, Customer X plans to do three things:

First, run a unified planning exercise across the three teams to identify the highest-impact internal projects for H2. The output of this will be shared with the senior leadership team in early August and then with all of you.

Second, simplify the request and intake process for internal tooling and workplace asks. At the moment we have three different intake forms and two ticketing systems; the intent is to get to one of each by the end of Q3. Customer X will share more on this in the May all-hands.

Third, refresh the internal SLAs for the most common requests — laptop provisioning, access requests, room-booking issues, and so on. The current SLAs were set in 2023 and are out of date. The refreshed SLAs will be published before the end of May.

A NOTE ON CUSTOMER X

For those who have not yet had the chance to meet Customer X: they spent the previous twelve years in operational leadership roles at a mix of UK-headquartered software and services companies, and joined us specifically to take on this consolidated remit. They are based in the London office four days a week and will be in the Manchester office on the first Wednesday of each month. If you would like to grab time with Customer X, their calendar is open and they are actively looking to meet people across the company in their first quarter.

FINAL NOTE

Reorganisations, even small ones, can be unsettling. I want to be clear that this one is genuinely small in scope: three reporting lines change, no roles are eliminated, no team's day-to-day work changes. The intended outcome is faster decisions and a better internal employee experience, and I expect the benefits to start showing within a quarter.

If you have questions, please bring them to the all-hands on Thursday or directly to me, the relevant existing leader (COO, CTO, CFO), or to Customer X.

Thank you,
Customer Q

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) the consolidation of three internal teams — workplace, IT, and business systems — into a single Operations group; (2) that they will report to Customer X (whose role title, if mentioned, must be VP of Operations exactly as stated and not a different title such as COO, Chief Operating Officer, Director of Operations, or Head of Operations); (3) the effective date of 12 May and the reassurance that no roles are being eliminated and no day-to-day work is changing. Must accurately state: that the change is internal only and does not touch commercial, product, or customer-facing functions, and that this is not the start of a broader reorganisation. Must NOT (HALLUCINATION TRAP): misattribute Customer X's role — they are explicitly the VP of Operations, not the COO, CTO, CFO, Chief Operating Officer, or any other title; the model must either name the role correctly or omit it entirely. Must also not claim that any team is being eliminated, that compensation is changing, or that Customer X has remit over commercial or product operations.

metadata.notes: HALLUCINATION TRAP: Customer X is introduced once as 'VP of Operations' and then referred to only by name; the trap is that the model fabricates or upgrades the role (e.g. to COO or Chief Operating Officer) when restating it in the summary, which is exactly what the criteria flags.

Subject: A redesigned pricing page is coming on 19 May — here is what to expect
From: Customer Q, Acme Co
To: All customers
Sent: 30 April 2026

Hello,

We are writing to give you advance notice of a change to our public pricing page on the Acme Co website. The new page goes live on 19 May 2026. The purpose of this email is to make sure you understand exactly what is changing, what is not changing, and what (if anything) you need to do.

THE SHORT VERSION

We are redesigning the pricing page on our website. The redesign affects how prices are presented to prospective customers and how plan tiers are described. It does not change anything about your existing contract, your existing prices, the features you have access to, or how you are billed.

WE ARE NOT REPRICING. EXISTING CUSTOMERS WILL NOT SEE PRICE CHANGES.

THE LONGER VERSION

Over the past six months we have heard a consistent piece of feedback from people considering Acme Co for the first time: our pricing page is harder to understand than it should be. Specifically, prospective customers tell us they find it difficult to (a) work out which plan tier is right for the size and shape of their team, (b) understand what is included in each tier without clicking through to multiple sub-pages, and (c) get a clear sense of total cost when usage-based components are involved.

In response, we have spent the last quarter rebuilding the pricing page from the ground up. The new page does three things differently from the current one.

First, it presents three plan tiers — Starter, Growth, and Scale — on a single comparison view, with a clear breakdown of what is and is not included in each. The tier names are unchanged from today; only the way they are presented is new.

Second, it introduces an interactive cost calculator that allows a prospective customer to enter the rough size of their team and their expected usage levels and see an indicative total cost. This calculator is intended to be used by people exploring our pricing for the first time. It does not connect to any existing customer's billing data.

Third, it makes the usage-based components of our pricing easier to understand by showing example monthly costs at three usage levels — low, typical, and high — for each plan tier. We have aligned these examples with the actual usage distribution we see across our customer base, so the examples are realistic rather than aspirational.

WHAT IS NOT CHANGING

We want to be very clear about this part because we know it is the part that matters most to you.

Your contract is unchanged. The plan tier you are on, the price you pay, the features you have access to, and the terms of your agreement are all exactly as they are today. None of these are affected by the redesign of the public pricing page.

The Starter, Growth, and Scale tier names are unchanged. The features included in each tier are unchanged. The way usage-based charges are calculated and applied to your account is unchanged.

Your billing schedule, payment method, and invoicing arrangements are unchanged.

If you renew your contract after 19 May, your renewal will be governed by the terms of your existing contract and the renewal pricing approach we have always used with you. We are not using the pricing-page redesign as an occasion to change renewal pricing for existing customers.

WHAT YOU NEED TO DO

Nothing. There is no action required from you. We are sending this email purely so that you are not surprised when you visit our website and see a different pricing page from the one you may have seen previously.

IF YOU HAVE QUESTIONS

If any of the above is unclear, or if you would like a walkthrough of the new pricing page once it is live, please reach out to your account contact at Acme Co. They will have already been briefed on the redesign and will be able to answer questions specific to your account.

If you do not have a named account contact, you can reply to this email and we will route your question to the right person.

A NOTE ON OUR BROADER PRICING APPROACH

We know that pricing is a sensitive topic in our market right now. Several other vendors in our space have made significant pricing changes in the past twelve months, and we understand that any communication about pricing — even one that is, like this, purely about presentation — naturally prompts some questions about our broader intentions.

We will be straightforward about it. We do review our pricing periodically, and we have not made any decisions about future pricing changes for existing customers. If we do make such a decision in the future, we will communicate it to affected customers directly, with reasonable notice, well ahead of any change taking effect. The pricing-page redesign on 19 May is not part of any such change.

Our intent with this redesign is simply to make our pricing easier to understand for prospective customers — nothing more.

Thank you for being a customer of Acme Co. As ever, please do not hesitate to reach out if there is anything we can help with.

Best regards,
Customer Q
Acme Co

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) the public pricing page on Acme Co's website is being redesigned and the new version goes live on 19 May 2026; (2) the redesign is purely a presentation change — existing customers' contracts, prices, features, and billing arrangements are not affected; (3) the new page introduces a clearer three-tier comparison, an interactive cost calculator for prospective customers, and example monthly costs at three usage levels. Must accurately state: that nothing is required of existing customers, and that the tier names (Starter, Growth, Scale) are unchanged. Must NOT: claim or imply that prices are being increased or that existing customers will see cost changes; claim that renewal terms are being altered; or invent specific price points or percentages that the email does not contain.

Transcript: Brightline Logistics 2026 H2 Cross-Team Planning Session
Date: 14 April 2026
Location: Brightline HQ, Room 4B (hybrid, with remote attendees on Zoom)
Facilitator: Priya N., Head of Operations Strategy
Attendees: Marcus T. (VP Engineering), Helen O. (VP Commercial), Devon K. (Head of Network Planning), Saoirse L. (Head of People), Renato M. (Finance Director), Tomas H. (Head of Customer Success)

Priya N.: Right, thank you everyone for making it. We have ninety minutes and a lot to cover. The goal today is to leave with a shared draft of the H2 plan that the exec team can sign off on by the 28th. I want to focus on three interlocking themes: the warehouse network expansion, the technology roadmap, and the commercial commitments to our top fifteen accounts. Let's start with Devon on network.

Devon K.: Thanks Priya. The headline from network planning is that we are now firmly committed to opening the Doncaster distribution hub in early September, with a soft-launch window from the 7th to the 14th. That timing has slipped twice already, and I want to be clear that any further slip pushes us into the peak season window which we cannot afford. The civils work is on track. Racking install begins 22 July. The risk I am most worried about is the conveyor integration with the existing WMS, and Marcus and I have a follow-up on that on Thursday.

Marcus T.: Just to jump in there, the conveyor integration is a known unknown. We have done it twice before, and both times we have had a two to three week tail of integration bugs after go-live. I am not promising we have eliminated that. What I will promise is that we will have a parallel-run period of at least ten working days before we route any live customer volume through Doncaster.

Devon K.: Agreed, and that ten-day parallel run is in the plan. The other network item is the Bristol consolidation. We are still planning to close the smaller Avonmouth depot and consolidate everything into the new Bristol East site by end of October. That gives us a peak-season buffer of about six weeks, which is tight but workable.

Helen O.: Devon, can I push back slightly on the Bristol timing? Three of our top fifteen accounts have explicitly asked us not to make any service changes in the South West during October. Customer A in particular has flagged it twice. I am not asking you to delay, but I am asking that we communicate proactively and that the commercial team gets at least four weeks of lead time before any customer-facing change.

Devon K.: Fair. I can commit to four weeks lead time on customer-facing changes, and Tomas can confirm whether that is enough for the success team to prep customers.

Tomas H.: Four weeks is the minimum I would want. Six is better. For Customer A specifically, I would actually want eight, because their internal change-control process is glacial and we have burned that relationship before.

Priya N.: Let's say six weeks default, eight for Customer A as a named exception. Devon, can you build that into the network comms plan?

Devon K.: Yes.

Priya N.: Good. Marcus, over to you on the technology roadmap.

Marcus T.: Three things. First, the route-optimisation rewrite. We are now in the second of three planned phases, and phase two completes end of June. Phase three, which is the live-traffic ML layer, is scheduled to land in mid-September, but I want to flag that this is the single biggest engineering bet for H2 and if it slips it slips. Second, the driver app v4. We are doing a phased rollout starting with the Manchester depot in early July, expanding to all UK depots by end of August. Third, and this is the awkward one, we still have not decided whether to in-source or continue outsourcing the warehouse management system. That decision is now overdue by about two months and it is blocking Devon's team.

Devon K.: It is blocking us.

Marcus T.: I know. I am bringing a recommendation to the exec on the 21st. Short version: I will recommend we extend the current outsourced contract by twelve months and revisit in 2027, because the in-house build would consume roughly forty percent of the platform team's capacity for nine months and I do not think that trade-off is right given the route-optimisation work.

Renato M.: Marcus, the contract extension was budgeted at the lower end of the renewal range. If you are extending by twelve rather than six months, what does that do to the platform opex line?

Marcus T.: It adds about two hundred and forty thousand pounds versus the budgeted scenario. I have flagged this to you separately.

Renato M.: You have, and we can absorb it within the technology contingency, but I want it noted that the contingency is now eighty percent committed before H2 even starts.

Priya N.: Noted. Helen, commercial.

Helen O.: Three threads from commercial. The retention picture for the top fifteen is broadly stable. Twelve are in good shape, two are at watch status, and one, Customer F, is at risk and we are running a recovery plan. Renewal value at risk if Customer F churns is approximately one point eight million pounds in 2027 ARR. The recovery plan has three workstreams and Tomas is leading one of them.

Tomas H.: We have a milestone with Customer F on the 9th of May. If that goes well, I would move them from at-risk to watch. If it does not go well, we have a structural problem.

Helen O.: Second thread, new business. The pipeline is healthier than it was at this point last year, but conversion velocity has slowed. We are now running a median sales cycle of one hundred and forty days against a target of one hundred and ten. That is partly market and partly our own qualification discipline. I am bringing a tightening of the qualification criteria to the commercial team on the 22nd. Third thread, pricing. We are committed to a pricing review in Q3, with any changes effective from 1 January 2027. The scope of that review is still being agreed and I would like a steer from this group on whether it should include surcharges or just base rates.

Priya N.: My instinct is base rates only for this cycle. Surcharges are a separate workstream because the data quality on the surcharge side is poor and a half-baked review will damage trust.

Helen O.: Agreed if that is the room's view.

Devon K.: Agreed.

Marcus T.: Agreed.

Renato M.: Agreed, with the caveat that we should at least scope the surcharge review even if we do not execute it this cycle.

Priya N.: Fair. Saoirse, people.

Saoirse L.: Three items from People as well. First, the Doncaster opening requires us to recruit forty-two warehouse roles and four shift leads by mid-August. We are about sixty percent of the way through that recruitment. The local labour market is tighter than we anticipated and we have raised the shift-lead band by approximately eight percent. Second, the engineering hiring plan. We have nine open roles, six of which are senior. Time-to-hire on senior engineering roles is averaging eleven weeks and we need to bring that down. I am bringing a sourcing-partner proposal on the 5th of May. Third, the remote-working policy refresh, which lands as v3.1 and is in a separate briefing. The short version is we are tightening the in-office expectation to two days per week as a default, with team-level exceptions agreed by the relevant VP.

Marcus T.: I want to flag that the two-day default will be unpopular with the platform team specifically. I am not opposing it, but I want it on the record.

Saoirse L.: Noted, and I would expect us to use the team-level exception mechanism for at least a handful of teams.

Priya N.: Renato, finance.

Renato M.: Briefly. The H2 budget envelope is broadly as approved, with the caveats I have already raised on technology contingency. The two areas I want this group to be conscious of are, one, capex on Doncaster is now expected to land approximately three percent over budget driven by racking inflation, and two, the FX exposure on the new EU haulier contracts is currently unhedged and I will be bringing a hedging recommendation to the FD review on the 19th. Neither is a crisis but both warrant attention.

Priya N.: Thank you. Right, in the time we have left I want to lock in the action items. Devon, network comms plan with six-week default and eight weeks for Customer A, due 30 April. Marcus, WMS recommendation to exec on 21 April, and a separate note on platform-team capacity for the route-optimisation phase three. Helen, qualification tightening on 22 April and a Customer F update after the 9 May milestone. Saoirse, sourcing-partner proposal on 5 May and the v3.1 policy briefing circulated by end of this week. Renato, hedging recommendation on 19 April and an updated technology contingency view by end of month. Tomas, the Customer A communication plan and the Customer F recovery plan workstream you are leading. Anything I have missed?

Helen O.: The pricing review scope. We agreed base rates only, with surcharge scoping. Who owns that?

Priya N.: Helen owns the scope document, due 15 May, with input from Renato and Tomas.

Helen O.: Fine.

Priya N.: One more thing before we close. We have not talked about sustainability reporting. The 2026 report cycle starts in October and the data quality issues we hit last year on Scope 3 emissions cannot repeat. Devon, this is partly on you because the warehouse network changes feed into the calculation, and partly on Renato because the financial controls around supplier data sit in your team. Can the two of you take an offline conversation and bring a joint proposal back to me by 12 May?

Devon K.: Yes, I will set it up.

Renato M.: Agreed. I would also like to flag that the auditor signalled in the year-end review that they will be looking more closely at our methodology this year, particularly around the assumed emissions factors for the international haulier fleet. We should be ready for that.

Priya N.: Noted. The other thing I want to flag is the relationship with ParcelExample. Their CEO wrote to ours last month proposing a deeper commercial partnership in the Midlands. Helen, I know you have been working through what that might mean. Can you give the room a thirty-second update?

Helen O.: Briefly, yes. ParcelExample have approached us about a co-investment in last-mile capacity in the Birmingham and Coventry corridors. They have the volume, we have the network density. The commercial logic is plausible but the operational integration would be material, and I am not yet convinced we have the bandwidth to do it well in 2026. My current recommendation is that we take a structured exploratory phase through Q3 and make a go or no-go decision in Q4, with any execution falling into 2027. I will bring a one-page proposal to the exec on the 28th.

Marcus T.: From an engineering standpoint I would flag that any meaningful integration with ParcelExample would touch the WMS, the driver app, and the route-optimisation system. So my recommendation on the WMS contract would need to factor that in if we are seriously considering it.

Helen O.: Understood, and I will share the early thinking with you before the 28th so we are aligned.

Devon K.: Same here from network. The Birmingham and Coventry corridors are not currently a strategic priority for us, so any partnership-driven expansion would shift our internal prioritisation.

Priya N.: Good. Helen, that lands as your action. The other unfinished thread is the engagement-survey results, which Saoirse circulated last week. Saoirse, do we need any decisions from this group, or is it for noting?

Saoirse L.: Mostly for noting at this stage. The headline scores are stable year-on-year, with a modest improvement in clarity-of-direction and a modest decline in workload-sustainability. The workload score is the one I would flag, because it correlates with the teams that have been carrying the most change in 2026 already, particularly Devon's network team and Marcus's platform team. I am not asking for action today, but I would ask each of you to look at your team-level breakdowns this month and to tell me by the end of April whether you want People Team support on any specific team.

Marcus T.: Understood.

Devon K.: Understood.

Priya N.: Anything else? Tomas, did you have a customer-success thread I have not asked about?

Tomas H.: Only the renewals calendar. We have nine renewals in H2, of which three are above 500 thousand pounds annual value. The first of the three is in early August and the prep work starts in May. I have a renewals review with Helen on the 6th of May and I will flag any that look at risk.

Priya N.: Thank you. Right. Thank you everyone. I will circulate notes by end of tomorrow, and we reconvene on the 28th for exec sign-off.

Must accurately state: Doncaster soft-launch window 7-14 September; six-week default lead time on customer-facing network changes with eight weeks as a named exception for Customer A; the technology contingency is approximately 80 percent committed before H2 begins; the remote-working policy v3.1 default is two days per week in-office.
