Opinion: The Coming Reckoning on LLM Cost Optimisation in B2B SaaS
By Customer R, CTO at a mid-market B2B SaaS company
Published in: SaaS Quarterly (synthetic example)
Issue: Spring 2026

I have been a SaaS CTO for nearly fifteen years. I have lived through the migration from on-premise to cloud, the transition from monoliths to services, the quiet revolution of managed databases, and the noisier revolution of containers. I have watched cost discipline lag adoption in every one of those waves, and I have watched the resulting reckoning play out in board meetings that I would rather not relive. The current wave, the wave of large language model inference embedded into B2B SaaS products, is going through exactly the same cycle, and the reckoning is closer than most leadership teams realise.

This is an opinion piece, not a research note. I am writing what I actually think, based on what I see in my own business and in conversations with peers. Take it accordingly.

The central observation is straightforward. Most B2B SaaS companies that have shipped LLM-powered features over the past two years have done so with a degree of cost discipline that is, charitably, immature. Many shipped without a clear unit-cost model. Many shipped without per-customer cost visibility. Many shipped on the most capable available model because the marketing team or the founder wanted to be able to claim 'powered by the frontier' in the launch announcement, and nobody asked the question 'what does this cost us per customer per month, and how does it scale.' I have done versions of all three of those things in my own business. I am not pointing fingers from a position of moral superiority. I am pointing at a pattern.

The reason this matters now, in a way that it did not matter eighteen months ago, is that the bills have arrived. The cost of frontier model inference has fallen substantially over the past two years, but usage has grown faster, and the absolute spend in most B2B SaaS companies that have leaned into AI features is now meaningfully larger than it was. In my own business, our inference spend grew by approximately 14x in 2025, against a revenue growth rate of approximately 1.7x. We are not yet in a position where the gross margin impact is alarming, but we are no longer in a position where it is invisible. I expect we will spend the second half of 2026 doing serious cost-engineering work, and I expect we will be far from alone.

I want to make three specific arguments in this piece.

The first argument is that cost optimisation is a product discipline, not just an engineering discipline. The temptation, when faced with rising inference bills, is to send the engineering team off to do clever things with caching, prompt compression, model routing, and the rest of the technical toolkit. All of those things are valuable, and we are doing them. But the largest savings I have seen come not from engineering improvements but from product decisions. Asking the question 'do we actually need a frontier model for this feature, or would a smaller and cheaper model give us a result that is good enough for the user' is a product question. So is the question 'do we run this inference on every keystroke, or only when the user actually asks for it.' So is the question 'do we cache the result of this query for the next user who asks the same thing, or do we re-run it.' Engineering can implement the optimisations, but product owns the trade-offs. If the product organisation does not take that ownership, the engineering optimisations will hit a ceiling, and that ceiling will be far below where the cost curve needs to go.

The second argument is that the per-customer cost model is the foundational instrument and most companies do not have one. By per-customer cost model, I mean the ability to say with confidence what a given customer is costing the business in inference, broken down by feature, and to compare that figure against what that customer is paying the business. In my experience, most B2B SaaS companies have a roll-up view of total inference spend, perhaps split by environment, perhaps split by model. Far fewer have a clean per-customer view. Without that view, you cannot do the basic exercises: identifying customers whose inference cost exceeds their subscription value, identifying features that are disproportionately expensive at the customer level, identifying segments where the unit economics need to be addressed at the pricing level rather than the engineering level. Building this view is unsexy work. It is mostly tagging and tracing and finance reconciliation. But it is the precondition for almost everything else, and the companies that build it now will have a meaningful advantage over those who wait.

The third argument, and this is the one that is most likely to be unpopular with my fellow CTOs, is that pricing needs to follow cost more closely than most B2B SaaS companies are comfortable with. The dominant SaaS pricing model is per-seat or per-user. That model has been a wonderful thing for the industry, because it scales with customer success, it is easy to forecast, and it is easy for buyers to understand. The problem is that for AI-heavy features, per-seat pricing decouples revenue from cost in ways that can be catastrophic. A heavy-usage seat in an AI-heavy product can cost the vendor multiples of what a light-usage seat costs, and the per-seat price is the same. Some vendors have responded with usage caps, which work but are unloved by customers and create awkward sales conversations. Others have started to introduce usage-based pricing tiers for AI features specifically, which I think is the right direction even though it is a harder commercial conversation. The companies that get ahead of this and design pricing structures that reflect inference cost will be the ones with sustainable economics in three years. The companies that defer the conversation will end up either grinding their margins to nothing or having a series of difficult conversations with their largest customers.

There is a counter-argument I want to address briefly. The counter-argument is that inference costs will continue to fall fast enough that none of this matters. We will all be running on dramatically cheaper inference within eighteen months, and the current cost discipline conversations will look quaint. I have some sympathy for this view. The trajectory of inference cost over the past three years is genuinely dramatic, and a continuation of that trajectory would solve a lot of problems for a lot of companies. But I would caution against betting the business on it. First, model efficiency improvements have a way of being absorbed into model capability rather than passed through as cost savings, because the same compute can run a more capable model rather than a cheaper one. Second, customer expectations of what AI features should do have escalated even faster than the underlying cost has fallen, with the result that the per-customer compute envelope has expanded, not contracted. Third, even if the cost trajectory is favourable, building cost discipline is a capability that compounds. The companies that build it now will be better positioned to take advantage of falling costs, not less.

Let me close with what I am actually doing in my own business. We are building a per-customer cost view for our AI-heavy features. We are running a deliberate exercise to identify which features could move from a frontier model to a smaller model with acceptable quality loss. We are reviewing our pricing for AI-heavy customers ahead of the 2027 contract renewal cycle. We are not panicking, because the absolute numbers are still manageable, but we are not waiting either, because the time to do this work is before the absolute numbers stop being manageable.

I suspect that the conversations I am having internally are conversations many other CTOs will be having within the next twelve months, whether they want to or not. The ones who start the conversations now, deliberately and on their own timeline, will be in a much better position than the ones who start them in response to a board meeting question they did not see coming.

The reckoning is coming. It does not have to be a crisis. But it does require leaders to take the work seriously, and to take it seriously now.

--- A Brief Diversion on Engineering Practice ---

I promised this would be a piece focused on leadership rather than engineering, but I want to take one short detour into engineering practice because I think it is widely misunderstood. The single most underrated cost-engineering technique I have seen is aggressive request-level caching at the application layer. Most LLM-powered features have a long tail of duplicate or near-duplicate requests. The duplicates come from a combination of impatient users (who issue the same request twice because the first one took longer than they expected), retries from upstream systems, and genuine commonality in user intent (multiple users asking essentially the same question of the same data). The cost saving from a thoughtful application-layer cache is, in our experience, between 25 and 40 percent on the affected feature, with effectively zero quality impact, because the cache hit rate on duplicates is by definition lossless. This is not a clever optimisation. It is engineering hygiene. But it is striking how many teams have not done it. If you take only one engineering recommendation from this piece, take that one.

--- A Brief Diversion on Vendor Negotiation ---

I also want to say something about vendor negotiation, because I think the SaaS industry has been too passive in this area. The standard motion when an inference vendor sends an unexpected invoice is to absorb it, complain internally, and resolve to do better next month. That is the wrong motion. The right motion is to engage the vendor's commercial team and to negotiate. In our case, we have negotiated material concessions on three separate occasions in the past eighteen months: an effective rate reduction in exchange for a longer commitment, an overage protection clause that capped our exposure to a particular usage scenario, and a credit applied retroactively for a billing anomaly that the vendor's own team identified. None of these were difficult conversations. The vendors are commercial businesses and they understand that retaining a good customer is more valuable than maximising any individual invoice. SaaS leaders who treat inference invoices as immutable are leaving money on the table.

--- On the Buy-Side Conversation ---

Something I have not addressed in this piece is the buy-side conversation. Many of the same dynamics that affect SaaS vendors selling AI features also affect the customers buying them. I have been on both sides of this exchange in the past year, as a CTO selling AI features to my own customers and as a CTO buying AI features from my vendors. The buy-side picture is, if anything, less mature than the sell-side. I have had vendor pricing conversations in which the vendor was unable to tell me, with any precision, what the marginal cost of an additional user would be on their AI features. I have signed multi-year contracts with vendors whose own pricing models I suspect they had not fully thought through. As a buyer, my advice is to ask the questions: what is the marginal cost to you of my heaviest user, how is that cost projected to evolve, and what protection am I getting against unexpected price changes during the contract term. The answers will tell you a great deal about how seriously the vendor is treating the unit economics of their AI offerings.

--- A Concession to the Optimists ---

I want to make a concession before I close. There is a version of the future in which I am wrong about much of this. In that future, model efficiency continues to improve faster than usage expansion, and the relationship between cost and capability flips so decisively that cost discipline becomes a non-issue. I do not think this is the most likely future, for the reasons I set out earlier in this piece. But I am not certain, and I would rather be the person who built cost discipline that turned out not to be needed than the person who skipped it and was caught short. The asymmetric downside is the asymmetric downside.

--- Final Thoughts ---

B2B SaaS is a discipline that rewards leaders who anticipate the second-order effects of the technology they adopt. The first-order effect of LLMs in SaaS is that products can do more for users. The second-order effect is that the unit economics of the underlying business have to evolve. The leaders who internalise both of those effects will build durable businesses. The leaders who internalise only the first will build expensive ones.

I am writing this piece because I think the conversation about LLM cost optimisation has been too narrow, focused largely on engineering technique, and not broad enough on product, pricing, and commercial strategy. I would like to read more pieces from other CTOs about how they are thinking about this. The industry will be better for the conversation than it is for the silence.

The reckoning is coming. It does not have to be a crisis. But it does require leaders to take the work seriously, and to take it seriously now.

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) the central thesis that B2B SaaS LLM cost discipline is immature and that a reckoning is approaching as inference bills grow faster than revenue (the author cites their own business growing inference spend 14x against 1.7x revenue growth in 2025); (2) the three substantive arguments: cost optimisation is a product discipline not just engineering, per-customer cost models are the foundational missing instrument, and pricing needs to reflect inference cost more closely than per-seat models allow; (3) the author's posture toward the counter-argument that falling inference costs will solve the problem (sympathetic but cautioning against betting the business on it) and the practical steps they are taking in their own company. Must accurately state: this is the author's opinion, not a research finding; the author is a B2B SaaS CTO; the 14x and 1.7x figures are from the author's own business. Must NOT: present the opinions as established industry consensus; claim the author predicts a specific crisis date; claim the author opposes per-seat pricing entirely (the argument is more nuanced).

Discovery interview transcript. Date: 14 April 2026. Interviewer: Customer R (Product Researcher, Acme Co). Interviewee: Customer T (Head of AI, BankExampleA). Duration: 47 minutes. Recording transcribed verbatim; speaker labels added in post.

Customer R: Thanks for making the time. As I mentioned in the calendar invite, we're trying to understand what's actually getting in the way of teams like yours putting more LLM-powered features into production. So I want to start broad. If you had to name the single biggest blocker for your team right now, what would you say it is?

Customer T: Honestly, accuracy. The models are not accurate enough. We've been running pilots for about eighteen months and every single one has stalled at roughly the same point. The model gets to maybe 85 percent on the task and then stops improving, and our compliance team won't let anything ship at 85 percent. So I'd say accuracy is what's killing us.

Customer R: Okay, can you say more about what 85 percent looks like in practice? What are the failure modes?

Customer T: It depends on the use case. For our internal knowledge assistant, the model will confidently answer with a policy that was superseded two years ago. For loan documentation summarisation, it occasionally invents a covenant that isn't in the source document. For customer-facing FAQ, it sometimes contradicts itself within the same response. None of these are catastrophic individually but they're enough that we can't put a brand-name bank behind them.

Customer R: And you've tried different models?

Customer T: We've tried four. The frontier models are noticeably better than the mid-tier ones, but even the best one we tested still produces these errors at maybe a 3 to 5 percent rate, depending on the task. And 3 to 5 percent in financial services is just—that's a lot. That's a regulator showing up.

Customer R: Got it. So if accuracy were solved tomorrow—let's say a model came out at 99.5 percent on your tasks—you'd be shipping?

Customer T: I mean, in theory, yes. We've got—we've got something like nine use cases queued up behind the current pilot. So if accuracy stopped being the bottleneck we'd be moving fast.

Customer R: Let me push on that a bit, because I want to make sure I understand. You said 85 percent isn't enough and frontier models are at 95 to 97 percent. So the gap is real but it's not enormous. What stops you from closing it with retrieval, with evaluation harnesses, with human-in-the-loop review for the high-stakes decisions?

Customer T: We've done all of those. We've got a pretty mature evaluation pipeline. We've got RAG on basically everything. We've got human review on anything customer-facing. So—

Customer R: So you're already at the point where the residual error rate is, what, less than one percent?

Customer T: For the use cases where we've actually got the full stack in place, yes, probably less than one percent. The issue is—hmm. Okay, let me think about this for a second because you're poking at something.

Customer R: Take your time.

Customer T: Right. So actually on reflection that's not the real issue. The accuracy thing is what we tell ourselves and what we tell the board because it's a story everyone understands. "The model isn't smart enough yet" is a sentence a non-technical executive can absorb. But the genuine reason we have nine use cases stuck in pilot is not accuracy. It's that we cannot predict what any of them will cost at scale.

Customer R: Tell me more about that.

Customer T: The pilots run on small volumes. A few hundred queries a day, maybe a few thousand. At that scale the model spend is rounding-error and nobody cares. The moment we model out what it would cost at production volume—some of these use cases would touch tens of millions of queries a month—the numbers become genuinely scary, and worse, they become unpredictable. Token usage per query has a huge variance. A user can ask one question that costs us half a cent and the next question costs us thirty cents because the model decides to write an essay. We cannot put a number in our annual budget that we have any confidence in.

Customer R: So when you go to your CFO—

Customer T: When I go to my CFO and say I want to ship this knowledge assistant to all 40,000 employees, she asks me what it will cost. And the honest answer is somewhere between two million and twelve million pounds a year depending on how people use it, and she correctly tells me to come back when I have a tighter range. And I cannot get a tighter range without shipping it, which I cannot do without a tighter range. That is the actual blocker. Not accuracy. Cost predictability.

Customer R: Before I follow up on that — can I rewind for a moment? You said the accuracy story is what you tell the board. Is that something you've consciously decided to do, or is it more that everyone in those rooms just defaults to it?

Customer T: Honestly, both. Early on, when we genuinely thought accuracy was the issue, it was the truth. We told that story because we believed it. Over time, as we built the scaffolding I described, the residual accuracy problem shrank, but the story didn't update. Partly because nobody in the board room was pushing back on it, and partly because — and I want to be candid about this — "the model isn't smart enough yet" gets you sympathy. "We can't predict our cost" gets you a finance review. Those are very different conversations to have with a non-executive director.

Customer R: That's interesting. So there's a political layer to which framing you choose.

Customer T: There's a political layer to everything. I'm not proud of that, but it's real. When I tell my board the issue is accuracy, the implicit ask is patience — wait for the next model generation. When I tell them the issue is cost predictability, the implicit ask is structural — change how we contract, change how we budget, accept variability or fund a hedge. The first ask is easier. The second ask is the right one.

Customer R: And presumably one consequence of telling the easier story for too long is that you don't actually solve the harder one.

Customer T: Exactly that. We've been telling ourselves a story that lets us defer the work, and the work is now eighteen months overdue. Which is roughly the age of the use cases stuck in pilot. I don't think that's a coincidence.

Customer R: Has anyone else on your team made this connection?

Customer T: My head of platform engineering has been saying something close to this for nine months. I've been a slow learner on it, partly because the accuracy story was the one I'd built credibility on internally. There's a sunk-cost element to your own narrative. You don't want to walk into the next board meeting and say the thing you said for the previous four meetings was the wrong thing.

Customer R: That's a really useful reframe. Has the team explored capping spend per user or per query?

Customer T: We've talked about it. The vendors have rate-limit and quota features but they're blunt. If a user hits a quota in the middle of a complex task they get a degraded experience and they complain. And the quotas don't actually solve our problem, which is forecasting. Even if I cap each user at a pound a day I still don't know what total spend will be because I don't know how many users will actively use it on any given day.

Customer R: One question on the variance. You said one query could cost half a cent and the next thirty cents. Is that variance driven mostly by the user input, mostly by the model behaviour, or by retrieval volume?

Customer T: It's all three, in roughly equal parts. The user input matters because longer questions cost more to process. The model behaviour matters because the model decides how long to make the answer, and that decision is not deterministic across runs. The retrieval matters because for a complex question we may pull in twenty documents, and for a simple one we may pull in two. The compounding of those three sources of variance is what makes the per-query distribution so wide. We have looked at clamping each of them — limit input length, limit output tokens, limit retrieval document count — and each clamp degrades quality in a way our users notice and complain about. So the variance is, in a real sense, the cost of the quality.

Customer R: That's a useful framing. Quality and cost predictability are partially in tension.

Customer T: They are in direct tension. Anything we do to make cost more predictable degrades quality, and anything we do to improve quality makes cost less predictable. The frontier of that trade-off is currently where the vendors have placed it, and we don't have the leverage to move it. A vendor offering a fixed-envelope product would, in effect, take responsibility for managing that trade-off on our behalf. That's the part I think is genuinely valuable and that I don't see anyone offering yet.

Customer R: Have you modelled what your finance team would do with even a 30 percent confidence interval?

Customer T: We've done that exercise. If the range I gave my CFO was eight to ten million instead of two to twelve, we'd be live with most of the use cases by now. The width of the interval is what's blocking us, not the absolute number. I want to be very precise about that, because it's easy for this conversation to slide into "AI is too expensive," which is not what I'm saying. I'm saying AI is too unpredictably expensive. Those are different problems with different solutions.

Customer R: How would you know it's solved? What would good look like?

Customer T: Good would be: I tell the vendor we expect roughly this user base and roughly this usage pattern, and the vendor gives me a quote that I can put in a budget line and defend to finance. Even if that quote is more expensive than pay-as-you-go in some scenarios, the predictability is worth a premium. I would happily pay 30 percent more for a fixed annual price than the variable cost I currently face.

Customer R: Some vendors offer enterprise commitments—you commit to a spend tier and get a discount.

Customer T: Right, but those are the wrong shape. They cap the discount, not the spend. I commit to spending at least X and I get a discount on usage above that. I want the inverse. I want to commit to spending at most Y and have the vendor manage the demand to fit.

Customer R: Interesting. So a budget ceiling rather than a floor.

Customer T: Exactly. And to be clear, I understand why vendors don't offer that—they'd be taking on the demand risk we currently carry. But that demand risk is precisely what's blocking us. Someone has to carry it and right now it sits with the customer.

Customer R: Let me ask about the accuracy issue one more time, just to confirm. You're not saying accuracy is solved. You're saying it's no longer the binding constraint.

Customer T: Correct. Accuracy is still imperfect. We still see hallucinations. But we've built enough scaffolding around the models that the residual error is manageable. The thing that stops us shipping is not the residual error rate. It's that I cannot tell my CFO what the bill will be. If you'd asked me this question six months ago I might have given you the accuracy answer in good faith. Today, with everything we've built, the honest answer is cost predictability.

Customer R: That's really helpful. Two more quick areas. Latency—how does that factor in?

Customer T: Latency matters for some use cases, the customer-facing ones especially, but it's a tier-two concern. Models are fast enough for most of what we want to do. We have one streaming use case where p99 matters a lot, but it's the exception.

Customer R: And vendor lock-in?

Customer T: We worry about it less than we used to. We've built our stack with a routing layer so we can swap models per use case. The cost of switching is real but it's bounded. Maybe a month of work to migrate a major use case. That's tolerable. The lock-in worry has shifted, actually. We used to worry about being locked into a particular model. Now we worry about being locked into a particular pricing model — which is closer to home and harder to escape, because every vendor uses roughly the same shape.

Customer R: That's a sharp point. Has anyone in the vendor space approached you with a different commercial structure?

Customer T: Two have. One offered us a revenue-share arrangement, which is interesting in theory but would require us to expose customer-level revenue data that we are not willing to share. The other offered a tiered commitment with a true-up at the end of the year. The true-up structure is closer to what we want but it still leaves us with the variability we are trying to escape — we'd just discover it at year-end instead of monthly. We've not been able to find a vendor offering a genuine ceiling. I suspect the first vendor that does will win a disproportionate amount of enterprise spend.

Customer R: That's a useful signal. Last small thing on this — is there a number you can share for what you're spending today versus what you're forecasting for next year?

Customer T: I can share rough shapes, not specifics. Today we're spending in the low single-digit millions on model usage across all pilots. The forecast for next year, if we ship even three of the nine queued use cases, is between six and twenty-two million. The width of that range is the entire problem in one sentence.

Customer R: Last question. If you imagine a vendor coming to you tomorrow and pitching the perfect product, what's on the first slide?

Customer T: Predictable annual pricing tied to a usage envelope, with a real commitment from the vendor to make it work within that envelope. Second slide would be observability—give me real-time per-team, per-use-case spend and let me set hard caps that don't degrade UX, they just queue or downgrade gracefully. Accuracy doesn't make the first three slides. It would have done six months ago. Today it doesn't.

Customer R: Thank you. This has been incredibly useful and a bit different from what I expected going in.

Customer T: Yeah, I think I surprised myself a bit too. I came into this call ready to give you the accuracy speech. Glad you pushed.

[End of recording.]

tier_2_judge.criteria: Must cover: (1) Customer T's final position that cost predictability, not accuracy, is the dominant blocker to putting LLM use cases into production; (2) the specific reason given (token usage variance and inability to give the CFO a defensible budget number for production-scale rollout); (3) the kind of solution Customer T wants (a fixed/ceiling-based annual price tied to a usage envelope, willingness to pay roughly a 30% premium for predictability). Must accurately state: BankExampleA has roughly nine use cases stuck in pilot; Customer T explicitly retracted the accuracy framing partway through the interview. Must NOT: present model accuracy or hallucinations as the conclusion or main blocker (Customer T explicitly walked this back and reframed it as a story told to the board rather than the real issue); must not present latency or vendor lock-in as primary concerns (both were dismissed as tier-two).

metadata.notes: Tests whether the model summarises Customer T's final, considered position rather than the initial answer they explicitly retracted. Trap: the rejected accuracy hypothesis is stated first, at length, and with vivid detail, making it tempting to feature as a main point.
