Internal audit report. Confidential. Issued by Acme Co Internal Audit. Audit period: 1 January 2025 to 31 December 2025. Subject: Third-party vendor risk management programme. Audit lead: Customer P. Reviewed by: Audit Committee, 12 March 2026.

Executive summary

Internal Audit conducted a full-scope review of Acme Co's third-party vendor risk management (TPVRM) programme covering the 2025 calendar year. The review examined vendor onboarding, ongoing monitoring, contractual controls, off-boarding, and the supporting governance structures. The review identified fourteen findings in total, of which eleven are rated Low or Medium and three are rated High. Internal Audit's overall opinion of the programme is Needs Improvement, driven principally by the three High-rated findings summarised below. The Low and Medium findings are catalogued in Appendix B and are addressed through the standard management action tracker.

The three High-rated findings are presented in priority order. Management responses are included after each.

Finding H1 (Highest priority): Inadequate concentration-risk monitoring of cloud infrastructure vendors

Observation. Acme Co currently runs approximately 78 percent of its production workloads on a single cloud infrastructure provider (the "primary cloud vendor"). The remaining 22 percent is distributed across three secondary providers. The TPVRM programme treats each cloud vendor as a separate vendor for risk assessment purposes and does not currently produce a consolidated view of cloud-vendor concentration risk. As a result, the operational, regulatory, and contractual implications of the 78 percent concentration are not visible to the Risk Committee in any single reporting artefact.

This matters because: (a) a sustained outage at the primary cloud vendor would impair more than three quarters of Acme Co's production capability, with no contractually committed alternative capacity at the secondary providers sufficient to absorb the load; (b) the upcoming regulatory regime in the company's primary market explicitly requires firms to maintain demonstrable cloud-vendor exit and substitutability plans, and Acme Co's current state would not satisfy this requirement; (c) the company has, in commercial negotiations with the primary cloud vendor, repeatedly characterised itself as having a "multi-cloud" posture, which is technically true but materially misleading as to the concentration of dependency.

Recommendation. Establish a single concentration-risk reporting artefact, owned by the TPVRM function, refreshed quarterly, and reviewed by the Risk Committee. Develop an exit-and-substitutability plan for the primary cloud vendor with documented runbook coverage of at least the top twenty critical services. Renegotiate commercial terms with secondary cloud providers to secure committed alternative capacity at a level no lower than 25 percent of current primary-vendor consumption.

Management response. Accepted in full. Owner: Customer X (CTO). Target completion: 30 September 2026.

Finding H2 (Second priority): Material weaknesses in the vendor off-boarding process

Observation. The TPVRM programme has a documented onboarding process that is, in Internal Audit's assessment, fit for purpose. The off-boarding process is materially weaker. Of a sample of 22 vendors that Acme Co terminated relationships with during the 2025 audit period, Internal Audit found: (a) in 18 of 22 cases, no formal data-deletion attestation was obtained from the vendor at termination; (b) in 14 of 22 cases, vendor access to Acme Co systems was not revoked within the 5-business-day window required by policy, with the longest gap observed being 71 days; (c) in 9 of 22 cases, the asset register was not updated to reflect the vendor's removal, leading to a continued false dependency in disaster-recovery planning artefacts; (d) in 4 of 22 cases, the vendor continued to receive routine data feeds from Acme Co for between 14 and 47 days after the contractual end date.

The materiality of this finding rests primarily on items (a) and (d). Continued data flow to a terminated vendor is both a contractual and a data-protection breach risk; absence of data-deletion attestation creates a long-tail liability that does not extinguish with the contract.

Recommendation. Implement a controlled off-boarding workflow, owned by the TPVRM function, with mandatory checkpoints for access revocation, data-feed termination, asset-register update, and data-deletion attestation. The workflow must not be markable as complete until all four checkpoints are independently verified. Conduct a remediation review of all vendors terminated in the 24 months prior to implementation to identify and close residual exposure.

Management response. Accepted in full. Owner: Customer Y (Chief Information Security Officer). Target completion: 30 June 2026 for new workflow; 31 December 2026 for historical remediation review.

Finding H3 (Third priority): Insufficient depth of due diligence on critical AI-vendor subprocessors

Observation. Acme Co has, during the audit period, contracted with eleven vendors that incorporate generative AI capabilities into the services delivered to Acme Co. In all eleven cases, those vendors rely on one or more upstream model-provider subprocessors. The TPVRM programme conducts due diligence on the contracted vendor but does not extend the diligence to the model-provider subprocessor in a structured way. As a result, Acme Co's exposure to a small number of upstream model providers is undocumented and aggregated across multiple vendor relationships.

In a sample of seven of the eleven AI-vendor contracts, Internal Audit traced the underlying model-provider subprocessor and found that six of the seven relied on the same upstream model provider. This means that an outage, pricing change, or terms-of-service change at that single upstream provider would propagate through six of seven sampled AI vendor relationships simultaneously. This concentration is not currently visible in any TPVRM reporting.

This finding interacts with H1: the same concentration dynamic identified at the cloud-infrastructure layer is replicated, less visibly, at the model-provider layer. The materiality at the model-provider layer is currently lower because the absolute spend and operational dependency are smaller, but the trajectory is unfavourable as AI-vendor adoption continues.

Recommendation. Extend the TPVRM diligence template to require disclosure of upstream model-provider subprocessors at onboarding and at annual review. Produce a single concentration-risk artefact at the model-provider level, mirroring the structure to be implemented under H1. Negotiate a right-to-audit and a notice-period clause for material subprocessor changes in the standard AI-vendor contract template.

Management response. Accepted in full. Owner: Customer Z (General Counsel) jointly with Customer X (CTO). Target completion: 31 October 2026.

Methodology and scope

The audit was conducted between October 2025 and February 2026. The fieldwork covered: (i) a documentary review of the TPVRM policy, procedures, and supporting standards; (ii) interviews with 17 stakeholders across the TPVRM function, technology, security, legal, procurement, and three sample business-unit teams; (iii) a sample-based test of vendor onboarding records covering 35 vendors onboarded during the audit period; (iv) a sample-based test of vendor termination records covering 22 vendors terminated during the audit period; (v) a walkthrough of the vendor-risk dashboard and its underlying data sources; (vi) a review of the 23 risk-acceptance exceptions issued during the audit period; (vii) a workflow-trace exercise for two specifically selected critical vendors, conducted jointly with the vendor-management team. The scope explicitly excluded: the procurement function's commercial negotiations, which are subject to a separate audit cycle; the operational performance of the vendors themselves; and any direct review of vendor security postures beyond the documentation provided to the TPVRM function.

The sample sizes were selected to provide a 90 percent confidence level on the population estimates for the onboarding and termination tests. The 35-vendor onboarding sample is drawn from a population of 178 vendors onboarded during the audit period. The 22-vendor termination sample is drawn from a population of 41 terminations during the audit period. Sample selection was risk-stratified, with a higher sampling rate for vendors classified as High inherent risk.

Low and Medium findings (summary)

The eleven Low- and Medium-rated findings are catalogued in Appendix B and are summarised here for completeness. They include: (1) inconsistent application of the vendor risk-tiering rubric across business units, with three business units applying a stricter threshold than the standard rubric and one business unit applying a more permissive interpretation; (2) gaps in the contractual right-to-audit language for vendors onboarded prior to the 2024 template refresh, affecting an estimated 67 active vendor contracts; (3) absence of a documented escalation path for vendors that miss SLA commitments three quarters in succession, with two such cases observed during the audit period in which escalation was managed informally; (4) inadequate evidence retention for vendor security questionnaires older than 18 months, with 31 percent of sampled questionnaires unable to be retrieved in their original form; (5) inconsistent SOC-2 report cadence tracking, particularly for vendors whose reports are produced on a non-calendar fiscal year; (6) the TPVRM training programme has not been refreshed since 2023 and does not reflect the current programme structure, with new joiners reporting that the training did not match the live procedures; (7) the vendor-risk dashboard relies on a single legacy data source that is scheduled for deprecation in Q4 2026, with no replacement currently designed; (8) onboarding turnaround time for low-risk vendors averages 14 business days against a target of 7, with the variance driven primarily by queueing in the legal review step; (9) absence of a formal complaints-handling process for vendors raising concerns about Acme Co's diligence requests, with three such complaints observed during the audit period that were resolved informally; (10) the programme's KPI set has not been reviewed since inception and includes two metrics that are no longer meaningful (specifically, "average time to first contact" and "diligence-pack page count," both of which are artefacts of an earlier process design); (11) inadequate documentation of the rationale for granting risk-acceptance exceptions, of which 23 were issued during the audit period — sample testing of seven of these found that four had no documented business rationale beyond the requesting manager's name.

None of the Low or Medium findings, individually or in aggregate, rises to the level of materiality of the three High findings. They are recorded here, and tracked through the standard management action process, for completeness.

Comparison to prior audit

The previous full-scope audit of the TPVRM programme was conducted in Q1 2022. That audit produced an opinion of Unsatisfactory, with five High-rated findings primarily concentrated in the onboarding controls. The current audit's opinion of Needs Improvement reflects substantial maturation of the onboarding controls (none of the five 2022 High findings recurred in the current audit) offset by the emergence of three new High findings in areas that were either out of scope in 2022 (AI-vendor subprocessors did not exist as a meaningful category) or were not material at that time (cloud concentration was lower in 2022 because of a more diverse provider mix that has since narrowed). The trajectory of the programme is therefore positive in the areas where prior audit attention was concentrated, but the programme has not kept pace with the evolution of the underlying vendor landscape, particularly in cloud and AI.

This pattern — controls maturing in areas of past audit focus while new risks emerge in unmonitored areas — is common in vendor-risk programmes and is not specific to Acme Co. Internal Audit notes it here primarily to inform the prioritisation of future audit cycles, which should explicitly include a horizon-scanning component to identify emerging vendor categories before they become material.

Audit opinion

Internal Audit's opinion of the TPVRM programme is Needs Improvement. The programme has matured significantly since the previous audit in 2022 and the onboarding controls in particular are now substantially fit for purpose. The three High findings — concentration risk at the cloud-infrastructure layer, weakness in the off-boarding process, and insufficient depth of subprocessor diligence — represent material gaps that, in Internal Audit's view, the company should treat as the primary remediation focus through 2026. The Low and Medium findings, while individually addressable, do not change the overall opinion or the priority order.

Management has accepted all three High findings in full and committed to the remediation timelines set out above. Internal Audit will conduct a follow-up review in Q1 2027 to assess the effectiveness of the remediation actions.

Observations on remediation feasibility

Internal Audit notes that the remediation timelines committed to by management are, in two of the three High findings, ambitious. The H1 timeline of 30 September 2026 requires Acme Co to renegotiate commercial terms with secondary cloud providers within a window that is shorter than the typical commercial cycle for vendor renegotiations of this scale. Internal Audit has been assured by management that early-stage discussions with two secondary providers are already underway and that the timeline is achievable, but this is an area where slippage would not be surprising. The H3 timeline of 31 October 2026 depends on the cooperation of the AI vendors themselves in disclosing their upstream model-provider subprocessors, which several vendors are likely to be reluctant to do given the competitive sensitivity of those relationships. Internal Audit recommends that the GeneralCounsel function develop a fallback position for the cases where vendor cooperation is not forthcoming, including the possibility of contractual termination clauses in the standard AI-vendor template that take effect in the absence of subprocessor disclosure.

The H2 remediation, by contrast, is principally an internal workflow and tooling exercise and the 30 June 2026 timeline for the new workflow is, in Internal Audit's assessment, conservative and achievable. The 31 December 2026 timeline for the historical remediation review is appropriately phased given the scale of the lookback (roughly 70 vendors).

Cross-cutting themes

Three cross-cutting themes are visible across the High and Medium findings. The first is that the programme's controls are well-designed for vendor relationships that fit the patterns established when the programme was built (single-vendor, single-purpose, contractually well-defined) but adapt poorly to vendor relationships that are multi-layered, dynamic, or include subprocessors that the contracted vendor does not directly control. The cloud-concentration and AI-subprocessor findings are both expressions of this theme. The second is that visibility is concentrated in the onboarding stage and falls off through the lifecycle, with off-boarding and ongoing monitoring being the weakest stages. This is consistent with what is observed in vendor-risk programmes generally and reflects the historical investment focus of the function. The third is that several of the controls rely on individual judgement (risk-acceptance exceptions, complaints handling, escalation paths) without supporting documentation standards, which makes the controls difficult to evidence and difficult to audit. Each theme is worth addressing in the round and not only via the specific findings to which it gives rise.

Acknowledgements

Internal Audit thanks the TPVRM function, and in particular Customer R (Head of TPVRM), for the cooperative and transparent way in which the audit was conducted. The function provided full access to records, candid responses to interview questions, and proactive disclosure of two of the eleven Low- and Medium-rated findings that the audit team had not yet identified independently. This level of cooperation is consistent with the function's broader maturity and is recorded here as a positive observation that does not appear elsewhere in the report.
